Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 2 A - Project identification A.1 Project identification Project ID (automatically created) CE0200647 Name of the lead partner organisation Politechnika Warszawska Name of the lead partner organisation (in English language) Warsaw University of Technology Project title Data Opener Central Europe Project acronym DoorCE Programme priority Cooperating for a smarter central Europe Programme priority specific objective SO1.1: Strengthening innovation capacities in central Europe Project duration (nr. of months) 30 A.2 Project summary Please give a short overview of the project and describe: the common challenge of the programme area your project is tackling; the overall project objective and the expected change your project will make to the current situation; what is innovative about your project; the main outputs and results your project will develop and who will benefit from them; the implementation approach you plan to take and why transnational cooperation is needed. All over the EU, various institutions gather data that is or should be openly available, which we call Public Open Data (POD). Currently, it is mostly available as static files (e.g. Excel or CSV) on local and national data portals. The related challenge we are facing, especially in Central Europe (CE), is that this data is often hard to access by society (citizens, SMEs). POD is truly useful only when it can be easily accessed through different digital services, like mobile apps. For instance, various open-data- related applications should be as easy to access as weather forecasts on a mobile phone. To develop such apps, innovative companies (esp. SMEs) require POD to be of good quality and dynamically available in real time. Unfortunately, according to EU reports, only a small fraction of citizens benefits from such digital services due to a lack of access to relevant data. Furthermore, even if such data exists, innovative companies (SMEs) cannot use it due to insufficient quality or lack of real-time (instant) updates. This has a significant negative impact on the innovation capacities to develop novel digital services for the society of Central Europe. DoorCE aims to tackle the current challenges related to POD in Central Europe by creating local Open Data Hubs providing high-quality, real-time data based on an easy-to-apply strategy and software tools.

# Page 3^page=3
Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 3 We take into account that regions and cities in CE have very varying knowledge and skills in opening and using public data. There are "newcomers" looking for good examples and "trendsetters" that also still face the need for increased innovation. Here, the transnational exchange in DoorCE can be the key to creating a "critical mass" required to equalise innovation opportunities between the CE countries. To address the challenges described above, DoorCE will focus on the following overall approaches: 1) transferring know-how (strategy and tools) to provide good quality real-time open data (e.g. by public authorities of various sizes), and 2) providing no/low-code tools for innovators (e.g. SMEs, citizen developers) to use this data effectively, even with limited IT skills. To implement these two approaches, DoorCE gathers a team of public institutions, business support organisations, SMEs and technology providers. This team will work together across borders to exchange experience and unify methods, considering the specific needs of stakeholders in Central Europe. This will involve developing a common and highly usable Data Opening and Security Strategy and making it widely available. The strategy will include models for managing, governance, provisioning and facilitating innovation capacities using open data. A novel Public Open Data Toolbox will accompany this. It will contain tools for data digitalisation, management, sharing and accessing. To make it accessible even to non-professionals, we will use the novel no/low-code approach. Thanks to this, it will be possible to quickly and easily publish dynamic data (via so-called APIs) and use this data in developing innovative digital services through local Open Data Hubs. The project activities start with developing the strategy for secure data provisioning and tools for creating web-based portals to access open data. In this, we will use gathered experience and existing background knowledge. We will use the strategy and tools to conduct the first pilot and initiate Open Data Hubs for partner public institutions. These hubs will become central points for each city or region to provide their data and promote innovation in using this data. After evaluating the first results, we will make improvements and add a strategy for facilitating POD innovation. We will also develop new easy-to-use tools for data digitalisation and real-time provisioning and usage, creating a comprehensive toolbox for open data providers and innovators. This will be validated in the second pilot, where Open Data Hubs will be extended with the new and improved strategies and tools. In parallel, we will develop and conduct relevant courses to transfer knowledge to parties in and outside the consortium. As a result, we will increase the number of public institutions providing good-quality real-time data, thus increasing the innovation capacities of SMEs to use this data in developing digital services. In the course of cooperation, we expect that public institutions of all sizes will use the outputs to increase and harmonise data provisioning and app development across borders, within Central Europe and beyond.

Please also describe what is the main business of the organisation and if the organisation is normally performing economic activities on the market. Warsaw University of Technology is Poland's biggest and highest-ranking technical university. The university's main goal as a public institution is to educate and innovate by researching and applying new technologies to the real challenges of society and business. Because of the technical nature of the university, it focuses mainly on the practical application of research and has substantial experience working with public and private organizations. The team (Software Modeling Group) involved in the project specializes in software engineering, focusing on low-code and no-code solutions that help make programming more accessible. We also have a lot of experience working with open data, primarily as the open data consumer, as many research and student projects are based on public open data made available by different institutions, including the Warsaw municipality. Additionally, we have experience with data preparation, cleansing, modelling, and provisioning for research and commercial use. What is the role and involvement (contribution and main activities) of your organisation in the project? The Warsaw University of Technology will be the Lead Partner of the entire consortium. It will also lead Work Package 2 dedicated to developing the open data toolset. Warsaw University of Technology will coordinate the consortium and, therefore, will be involved in most activities based on different areas of their expertise. The most significant part of the WUT effort will be developing the toolset. We have expertise in requirements elicitation, architecture design, implementation, and deployment. In their development effort, WUT will primarily focus on the Open Data platform and the functionality responsible for facilitating easier use and access to the open data with the help of low-code and no- code solutions. Additionally, the WUT members will support the preparation of the strategy (WP2) with their input regarding data governance and overall guidance regarding data quality necessary for them to be useful

Together they have many years of management experience, leading international projects, backed up by certification from different management and software engineering methodologies, including AgilePM, Scrum, Kanban, and Change Management. Please also describe what is the main business of the organisation and if the organisation is normally performing economic activities on the market. IKT Linz GmbH functions as the external IT department for the City of Linz (Associated Partner). Its primary responsibility is to provide comprehensive IT services to the city administration and the outsourced enterprises within the "UGL Linz" (enterprise group LINZ), excluding LINZ AG. The Open Commons Linz team within IKT Linz GmbH is dedicated to the provision of open data for the city. Their main focus is to ensure the availability of accessible and transparent data for public use. The city's data portal, data.linz.gv.at, serves as a redirection to the Austrian metadata portal, data.gv.at. It is worth noting that Linz holds the distinction of being the city with the highest number of datasets published on this platform. This demonstrates the city's commitment to promoting open data and fostering data-driven initiatives within the community. The City of Linz together with the Open Commons Linz team is actively looking for the possibilities to provide the open data dynamically and to build a community of innovators using Open Data in their region. What is the role and involvement (contribution and main activities) of your organisation in the project? The primary involvement of Linz is in providing their insights and expertise on open data sharing and opening, especially on the level of national and EU-wide regulations in WP 1 and 2. They represent a local authority already providing open data statically, only through the centralized government portal. Linz will participate in the pilots in WP 3, providing their experience in connecting to centralized government portals to less experienced partners and assessing the possibilities of creating Local Open Data Hubs.

Please also describe what is the main business of the organisation and if the organisation is normally performing economic activities on the market. The City of Zadar is a local government entity that encompasses the city of Zadar, along with four nearby settlements and seven islands. The city's responsibilities cover various fields, including spatial planning and construction, maintenance of communal infrastructure, issuance of building permits, social services, culture and sports, economy and business, EU funds management, environmental protection, island development, education, and other tasks specified by specific laws. Over the years, the city has fostered collaborations with numerous local government units and non- governmental organizations, both domestically and internationally. Through these partnerships, the City of Zadar has successfully implemented and coordinated numerous projects funded by various sources, including domestic and foreign financing. City of Zadar has implemented and/or is implementing a number of projects that synergize with the topic of the project, such as: e-Zadrani, GIS, Zadar Internet of Things, Property Register, E-office and web e-office, Zadar optical network, Zadar Urban Mobility 4.0. For instance, the goal of the e-Zadrani project is to digitize the services of the city administration and related public companies and to unify them on a common web portal in order to provide users with simple and easy access to local government services through a single web portal and smart devices. Moreover, the IoT (Zadar Internet of Things) concept allows people to interact with devices and devices with devices, integrating them into a network managed by web applications. By developing an IoT network, the City of Zadar provided the basis for the wide application of such technology based on open LoRaWAN technology. City of Zadar is not performing economic activities on the market.

Warsaw is the administrative, political and financial capital of Poland. Great accessibility, a number of talented, talented and educated young people, rapidly growing GDP and high quality of life make Warsaw the most attractive business destination in the CEE region. Currently, there are over 400,000 business entities in Warsaw, including international corporations and local companies. Thanks to the forward-looking strategy and high dynamics of development, global brands and start-ups feel comfortable and develop together with Warsaw. City Hall Warsaw has an open data portal that is the central distribution point for public sector data. It contains mostly dynamic data made available directly from source systems using API. Warsaw is considered one of the leading local authorities in dynamic open data sharing. The information provided is published in a uniform manner on the website https://api.um.warszawa.pl. The data is used by residents, companies and universities. They are the foundation of Smart City solutions and significantly contribute to the implementation of the #Warszawa2030 Strategy. The unit responsible for the open data platform, that has developed, maintained and improved the platform for the past 8 years, they have a significant experience in digitalization and provisioning of open data. This will allow them to create a universal tool for data digitization. This tool will allow for creation of digital resources of valuable information that are currently unavailable. The creation of a universal tool will reduce the number of maintained databases, reduce software maintenance and personnel costs, and will centralize information resources intended for digitization in one IT tool. This solution will also allow to improve the quality of the processed data and thus the usefulness of the solutions that use them.

# Page 64^page=64
Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 64 C.2 Project relevance and context C.2.1 What are the territorial challenge(s) that will be tackled by your project? Please describe which specific challenges and needs are addressed by your project and why they are relevant for the overall programme area, (please refer to chapter 1 and 2 of the Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE Programme document).

# Page 65^page=65
Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 65 Data provided publicly by various institutions we can call Public Open Data (POD). Typically, such data is used by other institutions for different purposes (e.g. statistics, reporting etc.). However, in some regions (esp. bigger cities), POD has become an important element of everyday life. Citizens can use such data regularly by, e.g. checking bus schedules, availability and accessibility of public facilities (offices, museums, even toilets) or air pollution levels. Such data is made available through publicly accessible web portals run by specific local authorities or public service providers and through mobile applications provided by independent developers (e.g. SMEs). In some cases, POD is made available to external entities dynamically in real time through so-called APIs (programming interfaces). This enables these external entities (esp. SMEs) to develop innovative applications serving citizens and improving their lives. Examples include an application for planning trips with public transport taking into account real traffic and positions of buses or an application to plan outdoor activities considering current (real-time) levels of air pollution. Making data available publicly poses a problem, especially for smaller public entities (small cities, rural regions). First, data has to be surveyed and analysed. Then, it needs to be "cleaned" and structured into a usable form, at the same time assuring the security of sensitive data (cf. GDPR). Finally, it has to be provided through a dedicated data portal, preferably connected to a national portal. Moreover, in order for the data to be available in real-time (e.g. traffic or air/water pollution data) for external applications, a secure API needs to be generated. This necessitates significant IT skills, which are currently limited on the market and usually unavailable to, e.g. local municipalities. As a result, local authorities do not provide POD due to a lack of know-how, lack of easy-to-use tools and fear of breaching data security rules. Another problem is associated with the usage of POD for the development of innovative digital services. Smaller companies and individual innovators usually lack the skills necessary to use the advanced capabilities of APIs. Moreover, data is often provided in different formats and using different API variants. This means that very significant effort and knowledge are needed to access POD and then use it to build an application based on an innovative idea. The above problems lead to certain challenges identified in the "Open Data Maturity Report 2022" (data.europa.eu). Generally, countries in Central Europe (CE) fall into all categories regarding their data opening maturity. We have "trendsetters" like Poland and Italy, but other countries are classified as "beginners" or "followers". Still, even in the most advanced countries, many smaller regions lag behind and still do not offer any POD. So, the imbalance in maturity is both between different countries and different regions within each of the countries of CE. Moreover, in the vast majority of cases, the provided data is of relatively poor quality (e.g. have different units, empty fields, vague meaning) and not dynamic (e.g. static files updated once a few months). Ultimately, the key challenge for the DoorCE project is to increase and equalise the capacities to provide and use POD across Central Europe. This way, we will support the process of digitalisation by strengthening capacities to develop innovative digital services (mainly - to be used in real life by citizens). In particular, this can be divided into two major challenges: 1. To facilitate the provisioning of POD dynamically (in real-time) by public institutions through providing necessary strategies and easy-to-use tools. 2. To facilitate the development of innovative applications (esp. by SMEs) that use POD to benefit society, e.g. in the areas of tourism, climate (environment protection) and smart city. C.2.2 How does the project tackle identified challenges and needs and what is new about the approach of your project?

# Page 66^page=66
Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 66 Please describe the project approach chosen to address the challenges and needs described above. Please also explain how the approach goes beyond existing practice in the sector/programme area /participating countries demonstrating the innovativeness of the approach. To address the challenges, we must significantly lower entry barriers for Public Open Data. For this purpose, the project will apply a bottom-up approach. We will start by identifying the actual needs of two major target groups: POD providers and POD users, at the level of individual regions in CE. Following this, we will develop a common set of tools (a toolbox) and an associated data-opening strategy. This will lead to the emergence of open data hubs, first - within the partnering cities and regions and then - around the CE. These hubs will have facilities to integrate uniformly with national and EU-level data portals. Key in our approach is the application of the so-called low-code/no-code paradigm. This novel paradigm will relieve POD providers and users (application developers) from many programming activities available only to professional IT developers. This will make our system unique through its accessibility and comprehensibility to personnel with little IT experience, thus enhancing capabilities for creating new, innovative digital services. Specifically, our actions will lead to developing a novel POD system composed of three elements: Public Data Opening and Security Strategy, Public Open Data Toolbox and Open Data Hubs. 1. In the Strategy, we will include detailed guidelines for public institutions of various kinds and sizes. We will develop instructions, descriptions of best practices and courses explaining the process of securely opening data by public institutions and in cooperation with external entities (esp. SMEs). Moreover, we will include instructions on activities that will enhance the usage of POD, especially by SMEs and other developers interested in developing innovative digital services. In all these activities, we will consider recommendations for improvement found in the "Open Data Maturity Report". Most importantly, we will align the Strategy with the use of the open data Toolbox. 2. To develop the Toolbox, we will start with well-known and used standard tools and specifications. We will consider open-source tools like CKAN and EU-recommended data exchange standards like DCAT-AP and talk directly to local institutions to identify barriers that prevent the wider use of these tools and standards in CE. We will consider ways to define data structures (so-called metadata) in a visual (graphical) way that is easy to comprehend. Ultimately, we will extend the existing tools with modules (plug-ins) that allow for automatically generating visual data representations into secure APIs and code to access these APIs from applications. This way, no advanced programming and IT skills will be needed to develop and maintain local data portals. The usability and comprehensibility of the newly developed Toolbox will be validated through the development of several data hubs. This will also provide feedback to improve ease-of-use and performance of the toolbox. 3. Open Data Hubs will be initiated at several partner sites. They will be created according to the developed strategies and using the developed toolbox. At the same time, they will provide all the innovative means for external entities (local public bodies, SMEs) to create their own data hubs or to provide their data through the existing hubs. For instance, a local municipality will be able to follow our strategy, install our toolbox and quickly instantiate its own data hub. Another example is a local business (SME) that would be able to quickly publish its real-time data (e.g. parking or toilet availability) through a local data hub. Yet another example is an SME that uses data from different regions to develop/extend a tourist app offering disabled people information about various facilities (parkings, toilets) in real-time (e.g. currently free parking places for disabled people). The data hubs will be created as part of two major pilots. In the first pilot, cities and regions will cooperate in developing data hub modules to provide and use data related to tourism and climate (environment protection). In the second pilot, the data hubs will be extended with modules to provide dynamic and real-time data in different aspects of a smart city.

Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 68 C.2.3 Why is transnational cooperation needed to achieve the project objectives and results? Please explain why the project objectives cannot be efficiently reached acting only on a national/regional /local level and describe what is the added value for the partnership and the project area in taking a transnational cooperation approach. Transnational cooperation is crucial for achieving necessary improvement in the provisioning and usage of Public Open Data across Central Europe. This is mainly due to significant differences between countries and regions within countries. Individual countries and regions do not have enough potential to create detailed strategies and easy-to-use tools for POD. In order to reduce the imbalance between the "trendsetters" and "beginners", cooperation across borders is the key. Specifically, several factors determine the need for a transnational initiative. 1. The development of strategies for POD has to be based on best practices and the hands-on experience of institutions experienced in POD. This knowledge is normally unavailable publicly and thus unavailable to the various institutions (esp. in smaller regions) that begin their journey in POD. Moreover, some countries do not have enough experienced institutions and organisational potential to provide good quality guidance for "beginners". We thus need to create an active forum to exchange this knowledge across borders and between various regions. This will enable the creation of usable and practical strategies for the provisioning and usage of POD, suitable for all the countries in CE and also considering the specific situation in each of the countries. 2. The development of new software tools is a very significant effort. Developing POD toolsets at a country level would necessitate many separate projects developing similar systems, which would mean a waste of resources. Moreover, these toolsets would not be compatible, preventing the future exchange of data and building transnational applications that use this data. Finally, in several CE countries, there is simply no potential to start a software development project dedicated specifically to POD. 3. The project's transnational value also consists in the emergence of an international community of application developers. The new POD strategy and toolset provide a unified ecosystem for software developers with templates, guidelines and tutorials. This will allow for the creation of a transnational community that would exchange information on ways to develop innovative applications for the benefit of the citizens of CE. It can be noted that limiting such a community to just a single country would not create a "critical mass" to gain the necessary acceptance, attention and potential for innovation. 4. The POD strategy and toolset will significantly enhance capabilities to use data transnationally through innovation initiatives to develop web and mobile applications that aggregate data from sources situated in different CE countries. This will be facilitated by using uniform, visual ways to define metadata that transcends the language barrier. Currently, such transnational POD usage is minimal. 5. Only a transnational initiative has the potential to raise awareness about POD in society, especially - among policymakers at the national and EU level. Public Open Data can significantly improve the everyday life of citizens across CE. However, this is not realised equally in all the countries and regions. Thus, only a transnational initiative can attempt to show all the benefits of POD in various areas of life, like public transport, tourism, environment protection, climate etc. C.2.4 Who will benefit from your project outputs and results?

Please indicate to which strategies and policies your project will contribute and briefly describe in what way. Strategy Contribution Other European Data Act (adopted by the EC on 23.02.2022) calls for setting clear and fair rules on access and re-use of data and investing in relevant next- generation tools. It also calls for joining forces in pooling European data in key sectors, with common and interoperable data spaces. This should be facilitated by giving users rights, tools and skills to stay in full control of their data. Our project directly implements this strategy in relation to open data offered by public institutions. Other European Strategy for Data - COM(2020)66, defines a major goal of creating a unified European data space, with the free flow of data. All citizens and enterprises (big or small) should be able to benefit from access and usage of non-personal data. The strategy calls for cross-sectoral (horizontal) measures for data access and usage, avoiding harmful fragmentation between sectors and countries. Our project directly implements this strategy in relation to open data offered by public institutions. Other Data Opening Programme for 2021-2027 (Polish Ministry of Digitisation) plans to increase the volume and quality of data available through the central data portal and develop an ecosystem to create new e-services and products and business models causing economic and social benefits. One of the key priorities is the increased availability of data through APIs. Our project directly responds to this strategy by providing tools facilitating access to open data through APIs. EU Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region The project contributes to Action 2: "Digital innovation and transformation". It directly responds to one of the main challenges under this action which is to "develop common standards for interoperable public and private solutions", and to "harmonise data" that will "support transfer of solutions across Baltic Sea region, new market opportunities and globally more competitive digital ecosystems". It also directly responds to one of the planned areas of activity which is the "Baltic Sea region open data initiative".

Please describe the experiences/lessons learned that your project draws on, and other available knowledge your project capitalises on. If relevant, please specify the projects to be capitalised and which project partner(s) have been involved. DoorCE will draw on the experience of project partners that are already advanced in instituting strategies and tools for opening public data. Warsaw maintains a project started in 2015 (Dane po warszawsku, https://api.um.warszawa.pl/ and Twoja Warszawa na bieżąco, https://iot.warszawa.pl/) to open various data sets (pollution, noise, parkings, weather, public transport, public bicycles, ...). Linz has significant experience in cooperating with the national data service (data.gv.at, https://opencommons.linz.at/open-data/) to provide their data sets (https://www.linz.at /digitaleslinz). Liguria Digitale provides numerous data sets on behalf of various local authorities in the Liguria region (https://www.regione.liguria.it/open-data.html). In all the countries, we will use the currently effective data opening strategies as the starting point for developing the common Strategy with detailed guidance for individual countries. We will also draw from the results of the currently finishing COM3 project (Interreg North Sea Region), where aconium participated in developing the methodology. This project resulted in strategies for the digital transformation of rural areas. Specifically, we will use the results of COM3 pilots that support digital entrepreneurship (incl. innovation) and use public data platforms as drivers for economic development. We will also build our Toolset on top of existing, mature open data tools, like CKAN (https://ckan.org /) and DKAN (https://getdkan.org/), already used by partners (Warsaw, Liguria Digitale). Such tools necessitate significant technical skills to operate. We will analyse their characteristics and draw on their technology and documentation. We will develop new compatible modules (plug-ins) that will facilitate defining open data structures, publishing dynamic data and generating software for easy access to open data from various (mobile) applications. We will also align this effort with the current standards for open data defined at the EU level. An important example is the DCAT-AP standard. It is a profile for data portals in Europe, based on W3C's Data Catalogue vocabulary (DCAT) for describing European public sector datasets. Its basic use case is to enable a cross-data portal search for data sets and make public sector data better searchable across borders and sectors. We will introduce new ways of defining data structures visually. For this purpose, we will draw on the results of projects like BalticLSC (Interreg BSR), ReDSeeDS and REMICS (6th and 7th Framework Programme), where WUT laid the foundations for visual, easy-to-use (no-code) languages. DoorCE will also draw on the experience gained through several EU-funded research projects in the area of "Digital Government: Co-creating innovative public services for citizens and businesses" (see the respective Results Pack at https://cordis.europa.eu/). Several of these projects, like "Big Policy Canvas", "Co-VAL", "OpenGovIntelligence", "WeGovNow" produced interesting results that pertain to strengthening innovation capacities in the area of public open data. We will examine the results of these projects (strategies, policies and tools) and analyse how to apply them in the context of Central Europe.

Please describe the structure of your partnership and why the involved partners are needed to implement the project and to achieve the project objectives. The consortium has been created to accommodate partners from multiple regions with different experiences and expertise in the field of open data to facilitate knowledge sharing and allow for a more universal approach to developed solutions. The core of the consortium consists of local public authorities (Velenje [SI], Sopot [PL], Zadar [HR] and Warsaw [PL]) responsible for provisioning public open data and public service providers who are providing Open Data services to public authorities (IKT Linz [AT], Liguria Digitale [IT]). The consortium is complemented by a large public university (Warsaw University of Technology - WUT [PL]) and private companies (aconium [DE], Trexima [SK], FOS [IT]) that specialize in supporting public institutions and provide knowledge regarding the use of Open Data by SMEs. The consortium will be led by WUT, which has experience in Interreg projects. The Project Coordinator successfully led an Interreg BSR project in 2019-22 with a total budget of over 2.400.000 Euro. Strong cooperation between all the partners will be required to achieve the end results, with each engaging more in different activities based on their expertise and needs. This will also involve several associated partners, including local (Bratislava [SK], Wien [AT], Linx [AT]), regional (Mittelsachsen [DE], Masovia [PL], Gdańsk-Gdynia-Sopot [PL]) and national (Federal Ministry of Finance [AT]) authorities, data centres (Usti [CZ], Federal Computing Centre [AT]) and support organisations (Budapest Institute [HU], Architectspl [PL]). The role of these partners will be to widen the scope of requirements and experience, extend dissemination possibilities and directly participate in selected pilot activities. The creation of the strategy will be led by aconium, who is experienced in supporting public authorities with the strategy and plans for public data opening and digitalization. The strategy will be jointly developed by the public authorities and SMEs within the project partnership (including associated partners). Partners (Linz, Liguria, Warsaw) have experience working with policymakers in the area of open data and its security. We will build on this experience, considering EU and regional policies. We will also use experience from the most advanced open data providers (Warsaw, Liguria, Linz), challenges expressed by the less experienced (Velenje, Sopot, Zadar), and insight from organisations (FOS, TREXIMA, WUT) working with open data once it is provided. The development of the toolset for Open Data Hubs will be the main focus of the more technical partners, with WUT having experience coordinating the development efforts of multiple partners in similar projects leading the activity. To develop this toolset, the development team from the City of Warsaw, which already has its POD platform, will lead the creation of a new tool for the digitalization of data, allowing connected institutions (both public and private) to provide data in digital format for the Open Data Hubs. The Liguria Digitale with IKT Linz will lead the development of POD provisioning system based on existing open-source components. Both partners already provide data in a static way by sharing regular files in CSV and/or XLS formats. They will work on developing a system allowing for dynamic (in-real-time) provisioning of data using the experience of the City of Warsaw, which already has such a solution. The last key component will be a universal, user-friendly web- based Open Data platform with Low-Code solutions allowing for easier access to dynamic Open Data without advanced programming skills. This will be mainly developed by WUT, which team specializes in Low-Code and No-Code web-based solutions with input from open data providers and users regarding the design and usability of the developed platform.

Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 86 C.4.1 Work package 2 Workpackage number WP2 Work package title Development of the public open data toolbox Objectives Please define one project specific objective that will be achieved by your project through the implementation of the work package. The specific objective should be: realistically achievable during the project lifetime; specific; be verifiable and measurable. Project specific objective The objective is to facilitate open data provisioning and innovation by developing an easy-to-use Public Open Data (POD) Toolbox. Requirements will be collected from providers (e.g. municipalities) and innovators (e.g. SMEs). The toolbox will be based on existing open-source tools like CKAN and data standards like DCAT-AP. On top of them, we will develop new modules that will use the novel concept of low-code (visual) open data provisioning and usage. This includes a visual designer for data structures, low-code API and API access generators, tools to prepare data in digital form and user interface templates. The POD Toolbox will also apply state-of-the-art mechanisms to assure data security. It will also be accompanied by comprehensive documentation. We will conduct regular evaluations to ensure the toolbox's ease of use and comprehensibility. The results of these evaluations will be fed back to the toolbox development process and used to improve the developed modules. In addition, please define one or more communication objective(s) that will contribute to the achievement of the specific objective and include reference to the relevant target group(s). Communication objectives aim at changes in a target audience's awareness and behaviour. Communication objective(s) and target audience The first objective is to raise awareness of the availability of easy-to-use data provisioning tools and thus cause more and more cities and regions to open their data and invoke initiatives promoting the creation of innovative digital applications. This will be achieved by directly contacting and offering tools to local and regional public authorities, organising at least 3 round tables and workshops, and publishing tools and documentation on the project website. The second objective is to raise awareness of digital service providers by demonstrating tools at specialist conferences (at least 2) dedicated to open data. This will involve a demonstration of our Toolset and possible innovations that can emerge from using it. Activities

Activity 2.1 Title Development of public open data toolbox Start period Period 1, 1 - 6 End period Period 2, 7 - 12 Description The goal of this activity is to develop the first working release of the Public Open Data (POD) Toolbox. Generally, work will consist in cooperation between the development team led by WUT (WUT, Liguria, Warsaw, FOS) and the user team (Linz, Zadar, Velenje, Sopot). We will also involve some of the associated partners as users. The first step will be to elicit and analyse requirements for the toolbox. This will be done through various interactions between the development and user teams: workshops, interviews, joint application development sessions, etc. In parallel, and following this, we will map the existing tools (like CKAN) and open data standards (like DCAT-AP) onto the requirements. We will analyse the current capabilities of these facilities and innovate on how to increase their capabilities having in mind mostly ease of use and comprehensibility. We will consider applying novel IT paradigms like no-code/low-code development and state-of-the-art security mechanisms. Following the initial requirements and analysis phase, the development team will undertake software development activities according to the best practices of contemporary software engineering. We will apply leading methodologies like Scrum, OpenUP and AgilePM. We will follow an iterative lifecycle, thus increasing the potential for exploring new innovative features of the Toolbox. In each iteration, we will perform a detailed requirements analysis, design the relevant user interface fragments, perform architectural design, and implement (code) the relevant modules. This will be followed by technical (unit and integration) testing and test deployment in the test environment.

As a result, more public institutions (local municipalities, regional authorities, service companies etc.) will provide POD across the various areas of CE. Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 104 C.7 Project management and communication In addition to the thematic activities as described in the work plan, you need to foresee adequate provisions for project management, coordination and internal communication. C.7.1 How will you coordinate and manage your project? Please describe how the project management on the strategic and operational level will be carried out, including the set-up of management structures, responsibilities and procedures, as well as risk management. Please also explain how the internal communication within the partnership will be organised. The DoorCE project will be managed using a selected well-established methodology, e.g. PRINCE2 with AgilePM. Strategic project management will be based on the collaboration of the following bodies: a) Project Steering Committee (PSC), which will be composed of officially designated representative of each project partner; b) Project Executive (PE), which will include Work Package leaders (for WP1-WP3), designated by the work package lead partners and the Communications Manager. The PSC and PE will be led by the Project Coordinator (PC), officially designated by the lead partner (WUT). The PC will also coordinate the collaboration of the above bodies. The PC will be supported in its daily tasks by the Project Administrative Support, who will create and maintain a Project Handbook. It will contain partner contact details, including financial officers, communication procedures (including tool usage), document sharing tool guidelines, project timeline with an active calendar and other necessary guidelines. Additionally, the PAS will assist in preparing and distributing agendas for meetings and communicating minutes of the meetings. The Project Steering Committee is the top-level decision-making body responsible and has authority over the whole project. Its members will be specified at the project kick-off meeting. The PSC sets directions for the Project Executive and the entire project team, monitors workflow, manages risks, and is responsible for the quality of results. It bases its work and decisions on well-structured internal progress reports prepared by work package leaders and information from financial statements and internal budget planning. The PSC meets every six months to monitor and discuss the project's progress and make strategic and operational decisions. The Project Coordinator chairs the meetings. In case of any urgent decisions to be taken, the PC arranges for online meetings in between. Specifically, during the meetings, the PSC will: • Review and approve the financial status, and reallocate budgets if necessary. • Review and approve the regular project risk assessment. • Review and follow up on the project quality procedures, including internal communication. • Review the overall technical programme and the project's technical results. • Provide strategic guidance for the PE and the project team regarding intellectual property, external communication and promotion activities. • Resolve potential and actual disputes between participants not resolved at lower decision levels. The PSC acts as an arbitrator in the unlikely event of significant disagreements. In such cases, a voting system will be implemented, which will be based on consensus principles. The responsibilities and powers of the PSC are defined in more detail in the partnership agreement, which will be signed following the signing of the subsidy contract. The PE takes key decisions concerning the day-to-day implementation of the project, including preparation of detailed schedules of work and synchronisation of work across all the WPs, planning expenditures within the frames of the budget and in line with the schedule, taking various decisions concerning the employment of experts, organisation of public procurement, settlement of

# Page 105^page=105
Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 105 documents. The PE will have regular monthly meetings and ad-hoc meetings whenever necessary. Some of the meetings will be coordinated with the meetings of the PSC. The tasks of the PE include: • Operation of the project content management and implementation of the project content management structure, • Coordination of technical activities between work packages, • Innovation management, • Development and implementation of the project quality plan, • Management of project risks. The Work Package Leaders coordinate the Activities within their work packages and the interfaces to other work packages. For that purpose, they coordinate the development of detailed work plans together with the Activity leaders and all involved project partners defining the responsibilities, output quality criteria, and due dates. They are responsible for initiating Activities, progress monitoring, quality and risks management, and closure of Activities within the Work Package. The Activity leaders are responsible for the operational coordination of the Activities, including detailed activity planning with all team members from the involved project partners. They are responsible for communication within the team, progress monitoring, and due documentation of all draft and final outputs, supporting the WP leaders in preparing the official WP activity reports.

Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 106 C.7.2 Which measures will you take to ensure quality in your project? Describe the planned approach and processes for quality management, i.e. how the quality of deliverables and outputs will be monitored and ensured, and indicate the responsible partner(s). If you plan to conduct any type of project evaluation, please describe its purpose and scope. The quality in the project will be assured by the Quality Assurance role appointed by WUT. This role will maintain the Project Execution Guidelines and IT Development Guidelines. The Project Execution Guidelines will constitute a handbook for the project members where relevant best practices and criteria are defined to be followed in the project. These include: • Project communication procedures, including the usage of Internet tools (mailing lists, repositories, communicators, etc.). • Procedures for approval and quality control of the deliverables, including the guidelines for defining deliverable acceptance criteria. • Configuration management procedures, including conventions for document formats, naming and versioning, repository structure and security issues. • Change management procedures, including submission and processing of change requests. • Usage of project management tools. • References to any standards that need to be met. • Project assurance procedures, including auditing project management at different levels. The IT Development Guidelines will form a handbook for the team developing the POD Toolbox and Open Data Hubs. It will cater for the same areas as listed above for the Project Execution Guidelines. However, it will be distinct due to the specificity of IT development activities. This handbook will define the notation, technical process, and techniques to be used to deliver good-quality software and systems. It will ensure the necessary balance between agile development practices and the necessary formality caused by the distribution of the development team. It will adapt an iterative process to reduce the risks associated with the inherent uncertainty of IT development caused by changing requirements and technologies. It will also define the tooling framework for the requirements definition, design, implementation, testing and deployment of the Toolbox and Hubs, as well as means to share other artefacts, documentation and source code. The Quality Assurance role will actively monitor the above-defined project quality procedures. This will also include adapting them to the current situation in the project and possible changes in the project execution and structure. Part of this monitoring will be workshops (conducted especially at the beginning of the project) where project members will be instructed on following the project procedures.

Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 108 C.7.4 How do you foresee the reporting procedures for activities and budget (within the partnership)? Please describe the reporting processes at the level of partners towards the lead partner. Reporting of activities in the project will be done at four levels within the consortium. At the Activity level, appropriate procedures will be set by the Activity leaders. These will mostly consist in day-to- day reporting on the progress of individual tasks assigned to specific personnel and reported in the selected project management tool. At the Work Package level, Activity leaders will report summarised progress to the WP leader during regular meetings. These meetings will precede monthly Project Executive meetings during which WP leaders will discuss progress as reported in currently running Activities. At the Project Steering Committee level, reports from the Project Executive will be presented and analysed. Activity reporting will be closely associated with financial reporting. All the activities by partners will need to be approved at the PSC level to be included in the financial reports. The project financial management is organised by the lead partner (WUT), involving all financial officers from the project partners. The lead partner's main responsibility is ensuring the timely submission of the financial reports to the Interreg Secretariat and ensuring that all partner reports comply with the financial regulations as stipulated in the grant contract. WUT will appoint a Chief Financial Officer (CFO) to the project. This person will define financial management procedures as part of the Project Handbook. These procedures will describe the internal financial reporting system, correct usage of time records, procurement procedures for external services and equipment, preparation of the reporting templates in time and timely delivery of partner reports to the lead partner. The CFO will be supported by the WUT's financial team, which will conduct daily financial (accounting) tasks. The lead partner will also engage a public procurement expert to supervise procurement procedures in the project. Each partner will use its financial (accounting) personnel for financial management at the partner level and for reporting to the lead partner.

Project partners have significant interest and resources to continue supporting and maintaining the project outputs (Strategy, Toolbox and Open Data Hubs) beyond the project lifetime. The City of Warsaw, Liguria Digitale and Linz already have teams dedicated to developing tools and opening their datasets. They intend to take over the project's results and continue further maintenance and development work. They will be supported in this task by WUT, who will maintain the project's website by publishing new developments and also participating in further development of the Toolbox. Companies participating in the project intend to maintain the Strategy by using and updating it during their further cooperation with various public institutions in their respective areas of operation. All the Open Data Hubs developed within the pilots will be maintained by respective local authorities as these are their statutory responsibilities, according to respective local data opening strategies. The project will aim at extending the number of public maintaining Open Data Hubs beyond the project members. C.8.2 Lasting effects Outputs and deliverables should be made available and used by relevant target groups (project partners or other stakeholders) after the project's lifetime, in order to have a lasting effect on the territory. Please describe how the outputs and deliverables will stay available and will be taken up or upscaled by the project partners. All the deliverables and consequentially - outputs of the project will be available in the public domain. Software modules of the Toolbox will be provided using an open-source license. This will be aligned with the open-source licenses of the base tools used to develop the Toolbox. This will ensure the wide availability of the Toolbox to all interested parties - public institutions willing to provide open data and companies (esp. SMEs) and individual innovators willing to use open data to develop innovative digital services. The Strategy will be published as an open document, and all the course materials will be available online, free for use by interested parties (educators, students, pupils, etc.). The Open Data Hubs will provide open data to all interested parties. Moreover, they will allow for easy integration for those parties willing to publish their data openly. All the project partners will use these project outputs in their regular operations long beyond the project's lifetime. This will be ensured by instituting Open Data Hubs at the premises of public institutions partnering in the project. These hubs will constitute an important asset for these institutions, and thus the constituting Strategy and Toolbox will be maintained and upscaled, as explained in C.8.1.

Interreg CENTRAL EUROPE 2021-2027 CE0200647 - DoorCE Version 2.0, 2024-04-23 112 C.8.3 Transferability Please describe how outputs and deliverables could be adapted or further developed to be used by additional target groups or rolled out in other territories beyond the partnership. How will communication activities ensure that relevant groups are aware of the available outputs and deliverables to be used? The project's main outputs (Toolbox, Strategy) will be developed with a key goal of easy adaptation to the conditions in various cities, municipalities and regions in Central Europe. For instance, a local municipality in Croatia or Slovenia wants to institute an Open Data Hub. They could use the Strategy to analyse their data sources, download our Toolbox, and quickly start providing data to interested parties (SMEs, innovators, general public) after installing it. The Strategy will include specific guidelines for their country (including compliance with local strategies), and the Toolbox will have built-in compliance with the national data portal technology. Another example is an SME with an idea for an innovative mobile app for tourists that would cover countries in Central Europe. It would download the Toolbox for developers and quickly generate software modules to access data available through the various Open Data Hubs. Creating such an app would not be feasible without the availability of the Toolbox and the Hubs. Also, the time spent developing open data access would be minimal. To make the above-mentioned entities (local authorities, SMEs) aware of the capabilities of DoorCE outputs, we will undertake various communication activities as explained in the work plan. This will include organising various round tables, workshops and courses. We will also participate in specialist industry conferences where we will demonstrate the capabilities of the Strategy and the Toolbox. Several partners in our consortium are active in various national bodies that manage open data at national levels. This will be a good opportunity to talk to policymakers at different levels to promote the ideas and specific solutions offered by DoorCe to be applied on a wider scale.
